Equity Research · Initiation of Coverage
Communication Services — China Internet
2026-06-30
Initiation of Coverage

Tencent Holdings Limited

HKEX: 700.HK
InitiationChina InternetMega-cap · ~US$484B

Executive Summary

Tencent is a dominant, cash-generative China internet platform trading at a discount to its intrinsic value and roughly in line with lower-quality peers — a mispricing rooted in China risk aversion and an investment portfolio the market largely ignores. The base-case DCF of HKD 586 implies ~39% upside to the ~HKD 421 price, and even the bear case (HKD 513) sits above the market. Value should be realized through the continued shift toward high-margin revenue (games, advertising, AI-enabled monetization), a normalizing capex cycle that lifts free cash flow, and sustained buybacks — though timing is a live question, with the shares in a low-conviction downtrend.

Financial snapshot

RMB bn (unless noted) LTM (Mar-26) FY2026E (cons.)
Revenue 768.2 828.2
Adjusted EBITDA 344.5 371.0
— Adj. EBITDA margin 44.9% 44.8%
Profit attributable (IFRS) 235.1 246.7
Non-IFRS diluted EPS (RMB) — 30.44
Net (debt)/cash [+ = net cash] +146.9 +196.2
EV / Market cap (HKD) ~3.63tn / 3.78tn —

LTM = twelve months to 31 Mar 2026; NTM = FY2026E consensus. EV/market cap in HKD; financials in RMB.

Investment thesis

  • Intrinsic value ~HKD 586 (base DCF), ~39% above the ~HKD 421 price; even the bear case (HKD 513) is ~22% higher — and this excludes the ~RMB 985bn strategic investment portfolio (~HKD 112/share of unpriced value).
  • Trades broadly in line with lower-quality peers — EV/EBITDA 9.1x adjusted (~9.7x reported) vs a ~10.0x core/peer median; NTM P/E 11.1x vs ~10.1x — despite the highest EBITDA margin in the group (44.9%) and a 7.4% FCF yield.
  • Margin mix-shift intact: gross margin has risen from 43% to 56% since FY2022 as high-margin games, ads and video displace legacy mix; adjusted EBITDA margin has gone 34%→45% and is forecast to ~48% by FY2030E.
  • AI capex is the swing factor: capex stepped up to 16% of revenue (FY2026E), compressing near-term FCF, then eases to 12% by FY2030E — a self-funded investment cycle against a deepening net-cash balance, not balance-sheet risk.
  • Capital returns: ~RMB 73bn/yr of buybacks plus mid-teens dividend growth, funded entirely by organic FCF, with a falling share count.
  • Reverse-DCF: the current price embeds an ~11% WACC (a ~2pp China/geopolitical risk premium over the 9% base) — the thesis is a re-rating on de-risking, not on operational heroics.

Key strengths

  • Weixin/WeChat (1.43bn MAU) — the highest-frequency distribution layer in China and the cross-battleground moat for games, ads, payments and, next, AI agents.
  • Fortress balance sheet — net cash of RMB 147bn and rising, plus a ~RMB 985bn investment portfolio the DCF ignores.
  • Structural quality — 56% gross margin, 45% adjusted EBITDA margin, 70%+ FCF conversion, and ROIC ~18–20% comfortably above cost of capital.
  • Franchise dominance — world #1 in games, China #1 in social/messaging, #2 in payments (a stable duopoly), share-gaining in ads.

Key risks

  • China macro/regulatory and US-China policy — game-approval cadence, the US DoD "Chinese military company" designation, and AI-chip export controls — the dominant swing on the multiple.
  • AI-capex overrun — heavier-for-longer spend on unproven near-term economics compresses FCF (management now discloses operating profit "excluding new AI products").
  • Investment-portfolio volatility — listed-investee fair value swung RMB 673bn→547bn in a single quarter; the Prosus/Naspers selldown is a persistent technical overhang.
  • Timing — a weak tape (downtrend, price below a falling 100-day EMA) argues against chasing.

Competitive positioning

Tencent occupies the high-value distribution layer of China's internet: Weixin's ~1.4bn-user graph gives it a structural advantage across every battleground — games (#1), social/messaging (#1), payments (#2 in a stable duopoly), advertising (share-gaining), and cloud (#3, traded for profitability). Rivals compete battlefield-by-battlefield; none replicates the cross-platform engagement and data moat.

Entry verdict

Attractive on value, patient on timing. The base-case DCF (HKD 586) and roughly in-line multiples on best-in-class quality point to material undervaluation (~39% base upside; bear still +22%), with the investment portfolio as unpriced optionality. But the technical setup is adverse — price sits ~12% below a falling 100-day EMA in a low-conviction downtrend — so this is a value entry for patient, long-horizon capital: scale in near the HKD 411 support and defer aggressive adds until price reclaims the 100-day EMA (~HKD 486). This process sets no price target; the Attractive rating reflects the intrinsic-value gap.

1Company Overview↑ Contents

What Tencent does, how it makes money, and where the quality and the risks sit.

Key takeaways

  • High-quality platform economics. Tencent monetizes Weixin's ~1.4bn-user engagement across games (world #1), advertising and fintech/cloud; revenue is durable (subscriptions plus evergreen live-service games) and increasingly high-margin as mix shifts toward games, ads and video.
  • Central debate — the AI-capex payoff. The mix-driven margin expansion is proven; what is not yet proven is whether the step-up in AI capex (to 16% of revenue) converts into ad/cloud monetization before it structurally compresses free cash flow.
  • Defining risk — policy, not execution. China regulation (game approvals, antitrust, payments oversight) and US-China policy (AI-chip export controls, the DoD 1260H designation) are external levers that can cap monetization regardless of how well the business executes.

1.1Company Description↑

Tencent is a Chinese internet and technology platform that monetises the engagement of its Weixin/WeChat and QQ user base across games, digital-content subscriptions, advertising, fintech and cloud — selling virtual goods and subscriptions to consumers, ad inventory to businesses, and payment/cloud services to both.

  • Founded Shenzhen 1998; HKEX Main Board since 2004 (dual counter — 00700 HKD, 80700 RMB). Reports in RMB.
  • The Weixin/WeChat super-app (1,432m MAU, 1Q2026) is the distribution backbone; games (world's largest by revenue) and advertising are the principal profit engines, with fintech/cloud the scaled third leg.
  • LTM Mar-26: revenue RMB 768.2bn; Adjusted EBITDA RMB 344.5bn; Adjusted EBITDA margin 44.8% (calc.). (Tencent reports Adjusted EBITDA = EBITDA + equity-settled SBC; group-level only.)
  • Market cap ~HKD 3.78tn (~US$484bn) at 421.4 HKD on ~9.0bn shares (29 Jun 2026).

1.2Product Offering↑

Tencent packages distinct product families across its platforms, mostly free-to-use with monetisation via virtual items, subscriptions, ad inventory and transaction/usage fees.

  • Communication & social: Weixin/WeChat and QQ — messaging, social feed (Moments), Video Accounts, Mini Programs, Official Accounts.
  • Games: flagship titles include Honour of Kings, Peacekeeper Elite, Delta Force, VALORANT and PUBG MOBILE; international via Supercell/Riot.
  • Digital content: Tencent Video, Tencent Music (QQ Music), China Literature.
  • FinTech: Weixin Pay / Tenpay — commercial payment, wealth management, consumer-loan facilitation.
  • Cloud & AI: Tencent Cloud (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), HunYuan/Hy foundation models, the Yuanbao AI assistant and WorkBuddy/CodeBuddy agents.

1.3Key Reporting Segments↑

Tencent reports four segments; Games and Social Networks sit inside VAS, and "Online Advertising" was renamed Marketing Services from 3Q2024. Segment operating profit / EBITDA is not disclosed (group-level reporting) — the single biggest disclosure gap for the model.

  • VAS (49% of FY2025 revenue) — Games (Domestic + International) plus Social Networks (Video Accounts live-streaming, music/video subscriptions); the highest-quality, highest-margin revenue, with International Games (+33% FY25) the growth axis.
  • FinTech & Business Services (31%) — commercial payment (#1 in China), wealth management and consumer-loan facilitation, plus Tencent Cloud; a mature, sticky payments base with re-accelerating cloud (AI).
  • Marketing Services (19%) — advertising across Video Accounts, Weixin Search, Mini Programs/Moments; a structural share-gainer, gross margin >50% for 11 consecutive quarters.
  • Others (1%) — residual, including select investment-related items.

The multi-year segment revenue detail (sub-line splits and YoY) that a mix chart cannot convey:

Segment FY2023 FY2024 FY2025 YoY FY25 (calc.)
VAS 298.4 (calc.) 319.2 369.3 +16%
— Domestic Games 127.9 (est.) 139.2 (calc.) 164.2 +18%
— International Games 54.8 (est.) 58.2 (calc.) 77.4 +33%
— Social Networks 115.7 (est.) 121.6 (calc.) 127.7 +5%
Marketing Services 103.5 (est.) 121.4 145.0 +19%
FinTech & Business Services 201.0 (est.) 212.0 229.4 +8%
Others ~5 (est.) 7.8 8.1 +4%
Total 609.0 660.3 751.8 +14%

Read-through: VAS's 49% revenue share understates its profit share — games and ads carry above-group margins while cloud is lower-margin — but Tencent's group-only disclosure prevents quantifying it; the absence of segment profitability is the single biggest disclosure gap for the model.

1.4Geographies↑

Tencent is overwhelmingly a China business; international exposure is concentrated in games and a small, fast-growing cloud footprint, and a full group geographic revenue split is not disclosed.

  • International Games were ~32% of games revenue in FY2025 (~10% of group revenue, calc.); group-wide international revenue is not separately broken out.
  • International cloud grew >40% YoY in 1Q2026 off a small base; otherwise revenue, users and regulation are China-centric.
  • Geographic risk: revenue, policy and FX are dominated by China/RMB; the principal cross-border risks are US/China policy (export controls on AI chips, the US "Chinese military company" designation) rather than operating diversification.

1.5Revenue Model↑

A mix of consumer transactional spend, recurring subscriptions and enterprise usage/fees, with a large prepaid/deferred-revenue balance evidencing collection strength.

  • VAS: virtual-item sales and battle/season passes (transactional, games) plus content/membership subscriptions (recurring) — fee-based VAS subscriptions ~266m (1Q26).
  • Marketing Services: auction-based CPM/CPC ad pricing; increasingly closed-loop (click-through to Mini Shops/Programs).
  • FinTech: commercial-payment take rate (per-transaction), wealth-management fees (AUM-linked), consumer-loan facilitation fees; Cloud: usage-based.
  • Deferred revenue of RMB 112.5bn at YE2025 (110.3bn current) reflects prepaid subscriptions/virtual items — negative-working-capital characteristics support cash conversion (FCF/Adj. EBITDA 54% FY25, calc.).

1.6Customers, Distribution & Demand Drivers↑

A diversified, predominantly consumer base with no meaningful customer concentration; distribution is self-owned, and demand is tied to Chinese consumer spending and enterprise digitalisation.

  • Customers/distribution: ~1.4bn Weixin users plus advertisers across most verticals and enterprise cloud clients; owned platforms (Weixin, QQ, app stores, Video Accounts, Mini Programs) lower customer-acquisition cost. Direct sales plus self-serve ad tools (AIM+ powered ~30% of marketing-services spend in 1Q26).
  • Games demand: discretionary spend plus global expansion — China games market ~US$50bn (Niko Partners, accessed 2026-06-29); secular plus regulatory (approval cadence).
  • Advertising demand: China digital ad spend ~US$163bn (2026), ~13–18% forward CAGR (ResearchAndMarkets, accessed 2026-06-29) — cyclical (consumption) plus secular (inventory/AI targeting).
  • FinTech/Cloud demand: payment volume tracks consumption (mature ~7.8% CAGR); enterprise AI workloads drive cloud, +20–24% YoY in 2025.

1.7Cost Drivers↑

Cost of revenue (RMB 329.2bn FY25, 44% of revenue) is dominated by channel/content/payment costs; the margin story is mix-driven operating leverage.

  • Variable/channel: revenue-sharing with content and distribution partners, content costs, payment-processing/bank fees, and bandwidth/server costs (rising with AI/GPU).
  • Fixed/structural: staff costs (total remuneration RMB 130.7bn FY25; 2024: 112.8bn) and rising depreciation from AI infrastructure.
  • Margin mechanism: gross margin expanded to 56.2% FY25 from 48.1% FY23 (+810bps, calc.) as mix shifted to high-margin international games, ads and high-quality cloud — strong incremental margins, with AI capex/depreciation now the principal offset.

1.8AI Considerations↑

AI is both the largest opportunity and the main near-term margin risk, with Tencent positioned through proprietary models distributed via Weixin.

  • Opportunity: Hy3 foundation model (top-ranked on OpenRouter token usage; >40% inference-cost reduction); AI ad targeting (drives Marketing Services growth); games content velocity; Yuanbao assistant and WorkBuddy/CodeBuddy agents distributed through Weixin's ~1.4bn-user graph; Tencent Cloud AI services.
  • Risk: capex +16% YoY in 1Q26 (RMB 31.9bn) and rising depreciation compress near-term margins — management now discloses "operating profit excluding new AI products" (1Q26 non-IFRS op profit RMB 75.6bn vs RMB 84.4bn ex-AI), an explicit signal that AI is a present drag and an unproven-economics bet.

1.9Research & Development↑

  • R&D expense RMB 85.7bn in FY2025 (2024: RMB 70.7bn), ~11.4% of revenue (calc.; FY24 10.7%).
  • Growth driven primarily by AI staff costs and infrastructure — the income-statement counterpart to the capex cycle.
  • Double-digit R&D/sales is high for an internet platform (Alphabet ~14–15%, Meta ~mid-20s%; Alibaba lower — indicative).

1.10Suppliers, Employees & Key Assets↑

Key "suppliers" are content/IP licensors and compute hardware; the binding constraint is advanced AI chips under US export controls.

  • Suppliers: game-engine/IP partners (Riot, Supercell owned; third-party licensors), music/video content owners, and data-centre/GPU vendors. Supply-chain risk: access to advanced AI accelerators (e.g., Nvidia) is constrained by US export controls — a genuine bottleneck management cites in pacing GPU procurement.
  • Employees: 115,849 at 31 Dec 2025 (2024: 110,558), +4.8% YoY, reflecting AI hiring; total remuneration RMB 130.7bn.
  • IP/facilities: Weixin/WeChat and QQ (the core moat), owned game IP (Honour of Kings, VALORANT/Riot, Supercell), the HunYuan/Hy models; Shenzhen HQ and a growing data-centre network (PP&E RMB 149.9bn at YE25 vs RMB 80.2bn at YE24, largely AI infrastructure).

1.11Key Acquisitions and Divestitures↑

Capital deployment has shifted from acquiring to returning/recycling capital; the last five years are dominated by large in-specie divestitures rather than M&A.

  • 2022 — Meituan (3690.HK): ~US$20bn stake distributed in specie to shareholders, a deliberate portfolio wind-down.
  • 2021 — JD.com: large stake distributed in specie to shareholders.
  • Ongoing — portfolio recycling: reduced selected listed holdings; new investing materially slower since 2022, with proceeds redirected to buybacks and AI (Bamboo Works, accessed 2026-06-29). Bolt-on acquisitions (games studios) only; purchase prices not disclosed (logged as a gap).

1.12Management & Other Risks↑

Founder-led with a long-tenured, stable senior team; company-specific risks extend beyond the operating sections.

  • Management: Ma Huateng ("Pony Ma") — Chairman & CEO, co-founder (since 1998), ~8.8% economic stake; Martin Lau — President (since 2005), runs operations/strategy/investments; John Lo — CFO since 2012. No material personal litigation identified for the CEO, CFO or Chairman.
  • US "Chinese military company" designation: added to the DoD Section 1260H list in January 2025; the company calls it "a mistake" and is pursuing removal — reputational and prospective contracting/capital-flow risk, not an immediate operating ban (CNN, accessed 2026-06-29).
  • Prosus/Naspers overhang: the largest shareholder (~23–24%) continues a programmatic selldown — a persistent technical supply (partly offset by buybacks).
  • Investment-portfolio mark-to-market: listed-investee fair value swings (RMB 672.7bn → 547.1bn over 4Q25–1Q26) flow through equity/earnings and add volatility.
  • Concentration of control / VIE / geopolitical: founder-led with significant insider influence; China-internet VIE structure and US-China policy are standing risks for foreign holders.

2Industry Overview↑ Contents

The China-internet pools Tencent competes in, and where it is structurally advantaged.

Key takeaways

  • Structurally advantaged at the distribution layer. Tencent owns the part of the value chain where the margin pool concentrates — engagement and data (Weixin) — which advantages it across every battleground it contests.
  • On the right side of value migration. The growth pool is shifting to advertising and cloud/AI (ad market ~13–18% CAGR to ~US$266bn by 2030; cloud +20–24%), precisely where Tencent is gaining share (Marketing Services +19% FY25 vs ~13% market growth).
  • Regulation is the sector's risk premium. Game approvals, antitrust and payments oversight are the dominant external swing; the cycle has eased materially since 2021 (approvals normalized), a tailwind for the multiple.

2.1Industry Definition, Size & Characteristics↑

Tencent operates across five overlapping sub-markets of China's internet economy — interactive entertainment (games), digital advertising, mobile payments/fintech, cloud, and digital content — converging on AI and short-video/closed-loop commerce. The investable boundary is large-cap Chinese consumer-internet platforms.

  • Scale of the pools Tencent touches: China games ~US$50bn (mobile ~RMB 257bn / ~US$36bn, ~73%) (Niko Partners, accessed 2026-06-29); China digital ad spend ~US$163bn (2026); third-party mobile payments ~US$372bn (2025) (coinlaw.io, accessed 2026-06-29).
  • Character: secular-growth but maturing, heavily regulated, and consolidated into a few super-platforms with strong network effects. Cyclicality enters via consumer/advertising spend; the structural driver is monetisation intensity (ad load, ARPU, AI) rather than user growth, which is largely saturated (Weixin ~1.4bn MAU).

2.2Structural Trends & End-Market Demand↑

Growth is shifting from user acquisition (mature) to monetisation and AI, with the fastest-growing pools being advertising and cloud — precisely where Tencent is gaining share.

  • Digital advertising — ~13.3% CAGR 2020–2025, projected ~17.8% to 2030 (to ~US$266bn) (ResearchAndMarkets, accessed 2026-06-29); driven by short video, closed-loop commerce and AI targeting. Maps to Tencent Marketing Services (+19% FY25).
  • Cloud/AI — China cloud infrastructure re-accelerated to ~20–24% YoY in 2025 on AI demand (Omdia via Light Reading, accessed 2026-06-29). Maps to FinTech & Business Services.
  • Games — record China revenue in 2025 (Outlook Respawn, accessed 2026-06-29); growth increasingly secular/global. Mobile payments are mature (~7.8% CAGR). The dominant technology change is generative AI across ad targeting, content creation, agents and cloud.

2.3Industry Structure & Value Chain↑

Each sub-market is an oligopoly or duopoly with high entry barriers (network effects, data, capital), and Tencent sits at the high-value consumer-distribution layer of the value chain.

  • Concentration: mobile payments is a duopoly — Alipay ~54% / WeChat Pay ~42%, together >90% of volume; cloud IaaS is led by Alibaba ~33–36%, Huawei ~16–18%, Tencent ~9–10%; games is led by Tencent then NetEase. Barriers: network effects (social/payments), content/IP and live-ops (games), capital and chips (cloud/AI), and licences (game approvals, payment licences).
  • Value chain & margin pool: users → distribution platforms (Weixin/QQ, Douyin, Alipay) → content/merchant ecosystems → advertisers/enterprises. The margin pool concentrates at the distribution layer that owns engagement and data; Tencent occupies it via Weixin. The structural shift underway is AI moving value toward whoever owns both distribution and proprietary models.

Market structure

2.4Competitive Landscape & Positioning↑

Competition is by battlefield rather than whole-company; Tencent leads in social/games/payments and is a challenger in e-commerce-linked ads and cloud.

Battleground Main competitors Tencent position
Social / short video / time spent ByteDance (Douyin), Kuaishou #1 messaging (Weixin); Video Accounts #2 short-video by DAU
Digital advertising Alibaba (Alimama), ByteDance, Baidu, JD Top-tier, share-gaining on under-monetised inventory
Games NetEase, miHoYo, Game Science; global studios China #1; global #1 by games revenue
Mobile payments / fintech Ant Group (Alipay), UnionPay #2 (~42%) in a stable duopoly
Cloud / AI Alibaba Cloud, Huawei Cloud, Baidu AI Cloud #3 IaaS (~9–10%); PaaS #2 by revenue
  • Positioning: Tencent's cross-battlefield defence is Weixin — China's highest-frequency app (~1.4bn MAU) and the distribution layer for games, ads, payments, Mini Programs and (next) AI agents — a structural advantage rivals cannot replicate.
  • Share trend: ad share gaining (revenue +19% FY25 vs ~13% market growth, calc.); games share leading but pressured by miHoYo/Game Science; payments share stable; cloud share below Alibaba/Huawei and traded for profitability. Precise revenue-share percentages are not reliably disclosed (ByteDance private) → logged as a gap.

2.5Margin Dynamics: Bargaining Power & Key Constraints↑

Pricing power sits with the platforms that own engagement and data; for Tencent the investment-relevant levers are advertiser/consumer demand elasticity, regulation, and the AI-chip supply constraint.

  • Customers/advertisers: a fragmented consumer base and diversified advertiser pool give Tencent strong pricing power in ads (AI targeting lifts effective CPMs while ad load stays below peers) and games (inelastic spend on evergreen titles) — supporting the >50% marketing-services gross margin sustained for 11 consecutive quarters.
  • Regulation as the key external lever: game approvals (NPPA), platform-antitrust rules, and PBOC oversight directly cap or release monetisation. The cycle has eased materially since 2021 (approvals normalised — ~1,600+ titles in 2025, >2,300 expected 2026; Niko Partners, accessed 2026-06-29).
  • Supply constraint that matters: access to advanced AI accelerators under US export controls bottlenecks cloud/AI scaling for all Chinese platforms — managed by pacing GPU procurement and prioritising internal workloads.

3Financial Overview↑ Contents

The margin, cash and balance-sheet trajectory — through an equity and a credit lens.

Key takeaways

  • Equity — a margin and returns inflection. Gross margin has risen ~1,300bp to 56% since FY2022 and adjusted EBITDA has nearly doubled to RMB 336bn; ROIC (~18%) sits well above cost of capital, and the adjusted-EBITDA margin is forecast to ~48% by FY2030E.
  • Credit — irrelevant in the traditional sense. The group runs a deepening net-cash position (net debt/EBITDA −0.4x LTM, extending further negative) with ~24x interest coverage; the question is capital-return pace (buybacks + dividends, ~RMB 110bn+/yr) versus reinvestment, not leverage.
  • The swing factor is AI capex. The step-up to 16% of revenue compresses FCF conversion to ~56% near-term (from ~70%) before it recovers — near-term FCF is a capex choice, not a demand problem.

3.1Financial Snapshot↑

Tencent enters the forecast period as a structurally higher-margin, fortress-balance-sheet compounder: a decade-long mix shift toward games, advertising and video has lifted the gross margin from the low-40s to the mid-50s, operating leverage has doubled the operating margin since FY2022, and the group now carries a large and growing net cash position. The four panels track revenue and growth, adjusted EBITDA and margin, free cash flow and its conversion, and the net-cash trajectory.

Financial summary

3.2Summary Financials↑

The full summary — operating KPIs, P&L, cash flow (with the working-capital sub-block), balance sheet and key credit/return metrics — across five fiscal years, the LTM bridge and five projection years. Every cell ties to the Excel model by construction.

($MM unless noted)FY2022FY2023FY2024FY2025LTMFY2026EFY2027EFY2028EFY2029EFY2030E

3.3Financial Trend Assessment & Analysis↑

Tencent's financial trajectory is best read as three reinforcing stories: a revenue-mix upgrade toward high-incremental-margin businesses, the resulting margin and returns inflection, and a cash-generation and balance-sheet profile closer to a mature cash compounder than a growth platform. Adjusted (company non-IFRS) measures are the analytical default; the GAAP-to-adjusted gap is bridged once below.

3.3.1Multi-year trends↑

Revenue compounded at roughly 10.6% over FY2022–FY2025 (554.6bn → 751.8bn), but earnings power grew far faster: gross profit rose from 238.7bn to 422.6bn as the gross margin expanded ~1,300bp to 56.2%, and adjusted EBITDA nearly doubled from 189.0bn to 336.4bn (margin 34.1% → 44.8%). The driver is mix, not price — the fastest-growing segments (games within VAS, Marketing Services, video/mini-program monetization) also carry the highest incremental margins.

  • Revenue & mix: VAS remains the largest segment (369.3bn, 49% of FY2025 revenue) with the highest gross margin (60.2%); Marketing Services is the growth standout (+19% YoY, gross margin 57.5%); FinTech & Business Services (229.4bn, 31%) is the slower, lower-margin (50.8%) but cash-generative utility layer.
  • GAAP↔adjusted bridge (stated once): FY2025 IFRS profit attributable was 224.8bn vs company non-IFRS 259.6bn; the ~34.8bn gap is overwhelmingly non-cash/investment items — equity-settled SBC (34.7bn add-back), intangible amortization and impairments, net of investee gains/losses and tax — and is recurring in character.
  • Margin & earnings: operating margin rose from 20.0% (FY2022) to 32.1% (FY2025) and non-IFRS diluted EPS from 16.32 (FY2023) to 27.88 (FY2025); ROIC improved from 11.5% to 17.7%, above any reasonable cost of capital — though the large investment portfolio (associates 342bn; FVOCI/FVPL >600bn) dilutes reported invested-capital returns and is better viewed as a separate store of value.
  • Cash & balance sheet: the group swung from net debt of 14.8bn (FY2022) to net cash of 107.1bn (FY2025), funded entirely by organic FCF (128bn → 224bn). Deleveraging was organic, not divestiture-funded.

3.3.2Most-recent interim↑

The March-2026 quarter extended the FY2025 pattern: revenue of 196.5bn (+9.1% YoY) with operating profit up 17% to 67.4bn and adjusted EBITDA of 89.6bn (46% margin) — profit growth well ahead of the top line as high-margin revenue mixes up. Quarterly CFO of 101.4bn drove net cash up sequentially to a record 146.9bn.

  • Growth mix: Marketing Services (+20% to 38.2bn) again led, followed by FinTech & BS (+9%) and VAS (+4%).
  • Operating KPIs: Weixin & WeChat combined MAU reached 1,432mn (+2% YoY); QQ mobile MAU continued its structural decline (516mn); fee-based VAS subscriptions were broadly flat at 266mn — monetization intensity, not user count, is the swing factor.
  • Read-through: nothing in the quarter challenges the base case; the record net-cash build reinforces the capital-return capacity underpinning the equity story.

3.3.3Most-recent fiscal year↑

FY2025 was a high-quality year: revenue +13.9%, gross profit +21% and adjusted EBITDA +21%, with the gross margin expanding ~360bp to 56.2%. Management framed the year around "high-quality revenue growth" — games recovery and the scaling of AI-enabled advertising and video-account commerce.

  • Margin & earnings: the margin lift flowed through to non-IFRS profit attributable of 259.6bn (+17%) and non-IFRS diluted EPS of 27.88; buybacks (73.3bn) supported per-share economics via a declining share count.
  • Cash & returns: full-year CFO of 303.1bn against capex of 79.2bn left ample room for 37.5bn of dividends (+30% YoY) and 73.3bn of repurchases while still building net cash by ~30bn.
  • Capex inflection: FY2025 capex (79.2bn, ~10.5% of revenue) marked the beginning of the AI/data-centre investment cycle — the central swing factor for near-term FCF.

3.3.4Financial Forecasts↑

The base case carries ~10% group revenue growth in FY2026E (828bn, matching consensus), decelerating toward ~8% by FY2030E as the base matures, with the adjusted-EBITDA margin expanding a further ~290bp to 47.7% on continued mix and operating leverage. The defining forecast choice — per the analyst sign-off — is a materially heavier capex path (16% of revenue in FY2026E, easing 1pp/yr to 12% by FY2030E), which compresses near-term free cash flow even as absolute FCF still grows.

  • Base-case drivers: VAS +9.5%→+6%, Marketing Services +14%→+10%, FinTech & BS +9%→+10%; gross margin 56.8%→58.2%; operating margin held ~1pp below the prior draft at the analyst's request; tax 17.5%.
  • FCF & capital allocation: FCF (CFO − capex) of ~208bn in FY2026E rising to ~363bn by FY2030E; dividends grow mid-teens and buybacks are held ~73bn/yr. Undeployed FCF accumulates as net cash (no forced redeployment), which the DCF handles by valuing FCFF and adding only current net cash.
  • Bull / Base / Bear: terminal-year (FY2030E) FCF spans ~304bn / 363bn / 413bn; cumulative five-year FCF spans ~1,230bn / 1,419bn / 1,570bn.

3.3.5Guidance↑

Tencent does not issue formal numeric revenue or EPS guidance. Management's qualitative framework emphasizes high-quality (double-digit) revenue growth led by advertising, games and AI-enabled products; continued gross-margin benefit from mix; elevated AI-related capex; and sustained capital returns.

  • Consensus anchor (third-party): the base case is calibrated to sell-side consensus of FY2026E revenue ~828bn (+10.2%) and non-IFRS EPS ~30, against a "Strong Buy" consensus (45 analysts) and an average price target of HKD 708 vs a HKD 421 spot.¹
  • Capital-allocation read-through: with net cash building toward record levels, the binding question for equity holders is the pace of buybacks and any step-up in strategic/AI investment — not balance-sheet risk.

4Valuation & Entry↑ Contents

What the business is worth, and whether now is the time to buy.

Key takeaways

  • Verdict — Attractive. Base-case DCF HKD 586 (~+39% vs HKD 421), bear HKD 513 (+22%), bull HKD 652; trades in line with peers on EV/EBITDA (9.1x adj vs 10.0x core median) and NTM P/E (11.1x) despite superior quality — and excludes the ~RMB 985bn investment portfolio.
  • Mispricing driver. A China/geopolitical risk premium — the market prices an ~11% WACC (vs a 9% base) — plus zero credit for the investment book; re-rating comes from de-risking, not operational heroics.
  • Entry — value now, patience on timing. Attractive value but an adverse tape (downtrend, price below a falling 100-day EMA) — scale in near the HKD 411 support for patient capital and defer adds until price reclaims ~HKD 486.

4.1Overall Valuation Assessment↑

Tencent screens Attractive. The base-case DCF sits ~39% above the current HKD 421 price, the stock trades broadly in line with lower-quality peers on headline multiples despite the group's superior margins and cash generation, and neither the market price nor the peer multiples give any credit for the ~RMB 985bn (~US$145bn, ~30% of market cap) strategic investment portfolio.

  • Intrinsic — base-case fair value RMB 507.6 / HKD 586 (FCFF/EV), ~+39% vs HKD 421.40; scenario range HKD 513 (bear) – 652 (bull), so even the bear case is ~22% above the current price.
  • Relative — EV/EBITDA 9.1x (Adjusted) vs a ~10.0x core/peer median; NTM P/E 11.1x vs a ~10.1x core/peer median. Broadly in line to a slight premium — but Tencent carries the highest EBITDA margin in the group (44.9% vs 10–35%) and the strongest FCF, so "in line" understates the quality gap.
  • FCF yield vs cost of capital — LTM FCF yield 7.4% against a ~2.0% RMB (China 10-yr) risk-free, a ~5.4pp spread; below the 9.0% WACC on a static basis, but the gap is more than covered by ~8–9% forecast FCF growth and is depressed by peak AI-related capex.
  • Unpriced optionality — the DCF and the comps both exclude the investment book (~RMB 985bn) and the ongoing buybacks shrinking the share count; both are upside to the figures above.

Upside drivers - Re-rating toward peer/core multiples on continued margin mix-up, AI-driven ad monetization, or clarity/monetization of the investment portfolio. - Capex normalization (16%→12% of revenue) lifts the FCF yield through the forecast window. - Sustained buybacks plus a compounding net-cash position.

Downside risks - China macro/regulatory or geopolitical de-rating keeps the multiple capped — the market is effectively discounting an ~11% WACC (see Valuation Stress Test). - Games-approval cadence and ad-cycle sensitivity to weaker consumption. - Investment-portfolio marks and RMB/HKD translation.

Valuation verdict: Attractive — intrinsic upside (base +39%, bear +22%), in-line multiples on demonstrably superior quality, and a large unpriced asset base, only partly offset by a static FCF yield below WACC and a China risk premium.

Entry timing: Tencent is Attractive on value — the base-case DCF (HKD 586) sits ~39% above the ~HKD 421 price and even the bear case (HKD 513) is ~22% higher — but the technical setup is adverse: price trades ~12% below a falling 100-day EMA in a low-conviction downtrend, with weak momentum (RSI 44) and light volume. The depressed price near the 52-week low is a value entry for patient, long-horizon capital, yet momentum argues against aggressive adds; scale in slowly and defer further buying until price reclaims the 100-day EMA (~HKD 486) with an up-turning slope.

4.2Relative Valuation↑

Against its China internet peer set, Tencent trades roughly in line with the group on EV/EBITDA and forward P/E while posting the highest margins and among the strongest cash generation — a quality-for-price gap the headline multiples flatter rather than capture. Comps are presented in USD (the peer group spans HKD and USD listings); every multiple is computed from the inputs, and the subject uses management Adjusted EBITDA while peers use reported (Yahoo) EBITDA.

CompanyPriceUS$Mkt capUS$MLTM RevUS$MLTM EBITDAUS$MEBITDA mgn%EV/EBITDAxNTM P/ExP/FCFxFCF yld%Div yld%

Reading the table - Tencent's 9.1x EV/EBITDA is on Adjusted EBITDA; on a reported basis it is ~9.7x — essentially at the ~10.0x core/peer median, i.e. Tencent trades with the group despite far higher margins and cash conversion. - Forward P/E of 11.1x is a modest premium to the ~10.1x core/peer median — reasonable for the highest-quality franchise in the set. - FCF yield of 7.4% is second only to PDD's 9.7% and well above the peer median (~3%); Tencent and NetEase (6.1%) anchor the quality/cash end of the group.

4.2.1Data caveats↑

  • Core comps (weight these): 9988.HK (Alibaba), 9999.HK (NetEase), 1024.HK (Kuaishou) — the three "battlegrounds" (diversified platform/cloud, games, short-video ads) with clean, positive-EBITDA data. Broader read-across: PDD, 9888.HK.
  • 3690.HK (Meituan) is retained in the peer set (see Appendix) but excluded from the comps table — negative TTM EBITDA makes its EV/EBITDA and P/FCF not meaningful; reflects heavy new-initiative losses.
  • PDD: low 3.2x EV/EBITDA reflects a very large net-cash balance and reported-EBITDA basis; Temu losses and US-listing distort comparability — a read-across, not a core anchor.
  • Net cash across the group lowers EV; Yahoo's cash figures for these names often exclude short-term investments, understating net cash and overstating EV/EBITDA — treat peer EV/EBITDA as conservative (biased high).
  • EBITDA basis: subject Adjusted (model) vs peers reported (Yahoo) — modestly flatters Tencent's relative margin/multiple. Multiples exclude the ~RMB 985bn investee portfolio; USD comps converted per-field (trading vs reporting currency).

4.3Intrinsic Valuation↑

The dual-DCF (FCFF discounted at WACC, FCFE at cost of equity as a cross-check) values Tencent's operating business plus net cash at RMB 4,679bn of equity, or RMB 507.6 / HKD 586 per share — roughly +39% above the HKD 421.40 price. All figures are narrated from the engine artifacts; none is recomputed here.

Case Fair value / sh (RMB) ≈ HKD Upside vs HKD 421
Bear 444.0 512.8 +21.7%
Base 507.6 586.3 +39.1%
Bull 564.2 651.6 +54.6%
  • Bridge — PV of explicit FCFF RMB 943bn + PV of terminal RMB 3,589bn = EV RMB 4,532bn; plus net cash RMB 147bn → equity RMB 4,679bn ÷ 9,218mm shares.
  • WACC 9.0% (cost of equity 9.15%: Rf 2.0%, ERP 6.5%, β 1.10). The 1.10 beta is deliberately conservative — Tencent's observed beta is 0.74, at which value would be materially higher.
  • Drivers — revenue compounds ~9% (FY26E +10% fading to +8% by FY30E); adjusted operating margin widens ~210bp on mix and operating leverage; capex fades 16%→12% of revenue.

Three diagnostics frame the confidence in the number: - Terminal cross-check — the Gordon terminal (g 3.0%) implies an exit EV/EBITDA of 10.7x (10.0x on Adjusted EBITDA), essentially where Tencent trades today — the terminal assumes no re-rating. - TV % of EV = 79% — high, as expected for a compounder on a 5-year explicit window; it concentrates value in post-forecast assumptions, which the Stress Test flexes directly. - FCFF vs FCFE reconciliation gap = 9.4% — the FCFE cross-check (RMB 459.7 / HKD 531) sits below the FCFF value because the levered path carries interest expense on gross debt but not the interest income on Tencent's large cash pile; for a net-cash company the FCFF/EV value is the anchor and FCFE brackets the downside.

Two material simplifications, both conservative: the DCF excludes the ~RMB 985bn investment portfolio and does not deduct the ~RMB 89bn minority interest — the net effect understates intrinsic value by ~RMB 896bn (~RMB 97 / ~HKD 112 per share), so the RMB 507.6 base case is best read as a floor on core value.

4.4Football Field↑

Triangulating the intrinsic DCF against peer EV/EBITDA and forward-P/E multiples and the 52-week trading range, the current price sits below the DCF and EV/EBITDA-comps midpoints and near the bottom of its 12-month range.

Method Low Mid High
DCF (FCFF) — bear/base/bull 512.8 586.3 651.6
EV/EBITDA Comps 154.6 449.0 609.9
NTM P/E Comps 235.7 382.8 475.6
52-Week Range (market) 411.0 — 683.0
Current price 421.4
  • The comps-based methods straddle the price: the EV/EBITDA-comps midpoint (449) sits above it while the NTM P/E-comps midpoint (383) sits just below — the market pays roughly the profitable-peer multiple for Tencent's EBITDA and a touch above it on near-term earnings.
  • The DCF cluster (513–652) sits entirely above the comps and the price, reflecting long-duration compounding and the conservative inputs (β 1.10, investment book excluded).
  • The setup skews to the upside: the DCF and the EV/EBITDA-comps midpoint sit above the price, the P/E-comps midpoint just below, and the price is pinned near the 52-week low (411) — the downside is the trading range, the upside is the DCF.
  • Basis note: the comps bars apply peer reported-basis multiples to Tencent's Adjusted EBITDA / NTM EPS; the wide low/high on the EV/EBITDA bar reflects peer dispersion (PDD low, Alibaba high), so read the midpoints.

4.5Valuation Stress Test↑

The valuation's sensitivity to its two key judgment inputs — WACC and terminal growth — is bounded below by the current price: the market price corresponds to assumptions outside the base-forecast grid entirely.

Intrinsic value per share (RMB), WACC × terminal growth:

WACC \ g 2.0% 2.5% 3.0% 3.5% 4.0%
8.0% 525.0 563.9 610.5 667.5 738.8
8.5% 483.9 516.2 554.4 600.1 656.1
9.0% 448.7 475.9 507.6 545.1 590.0
9.5% 418.3 441.4 468.1 499.2 536.0
10.0% 391.7 411.5 434.2 460.4 491.0
  • The grid spans RMB 392–739 (≈ HKD 452–853); the current HKD 421 price equates to RMB ~365 — below every cell.
  • Reverse-DCF — to reproduce the current price on the base forecast, WACC must rise to ~11% (a ~2pp China/geopolitical risk premium over the 9.0% base) or terminal growth fall below ~1%; equivalently, the price embeds sub-bear operating assumptions (low-single-digit revenue growth versus the ~9% base). The market is pricing risk, not a deterioration the fundamentals show.
  • Terminal-value reliance — with TV at 79% of EV and an implied exit multiple (10.7x) already at today's trading level, the thesis does not depend on multiple expansion; it breaks only if the discount rate stays structurally elevated (persistent China risk premium) or growth/margins undershoot the bear path.

4.6Technical Analysis↑

Technical read as of 2026-06-30. Tencent is in a low-conviction downtrend: the price (HKD ~430) trades ~12% below a falling 100-day EMA, with weak momentum and light volume. Despite the Attractive valuation, the technical setup argues for patience on timing rather than a chase.

  • Trend — price sits −11.6% below the 100-day EMA (~HKD 486), and that EMA is falling (slope −2.5%): a confirmed downtrend. Bollinger %B of 0.30 places price in the lower-middle of the 20-day band — soft, but not pinned to the lower band.
  • Momentum — RSI(20) at 44 is weak (30–50 zone), below the 50 midline but short of oversold. MACD histogram at −1.87 is mixed-to-negative with no bullish crossover.
  • Volatility — unremarkable; %B 0.30 with a mid-width band, neither a squeeze nor a lower-band capitulation.
  • Volume — 111% of the 20-day average (roughly average/light): the drift lower is not heavy distribution, but there is no accumulation volume to mark a bottom.
  • Key levels — support at the 52-week low HKD 411 (immediate) and ~HKD 400 (round-number); resistance at the falling 100-day EMA ~HKD 486, the level to reclaim to flip the regime.

4.6.1Entry Assessment (combined)↑

Tencent is Attractive on value — the base-case DCF (HKD 586) sits ~39% above the ~HKD 421 price and even the bear case (HKD 513) is ~22% higher — but the technical setup is adverse: price trades ~12% below a falling 100-day EMA in a low-conviction downtrend, with weak momentum (RSI 44) and light volume. The depressed price near the 52-week low is a value entry for patient, long-horizon capital, yet momentum argues against aggressive adds; scale in slowly and defer further buying until price reclaims the 100-day EMA (~HKD 486) with an up-turning slope.

Preferred entry zone: HKD 411–430 (near the 52-week low — a value zone for patient capital; defer meaningful adds until price reclaims the falling 100-day EMA at ~HKD 486).

Key levels: | Level | Price | Basis | |---|--:|---| | Support 1 | HKD 411 | 52-week low; immediate support | | Support 2 | HKD 400 | round-number support below the 52-week low | | Resistance 1 | HKD 486 | falling 100-day EMA — the level to reclaim to flip the downtrend |

Technical analysis presented as research illustration only.

5Appendix↑ Contents

5.1Financials — Basis of Preparation↑

Reporting currency RMB, millions (RMB mn); market/valuation context in HKD. Framework: IFRS. Fiscal year-end 31 December. Reporting cadence: semi-annual interim report plus quarterly results announcements (full IS/BS/CFS at H1 and FY; condensed at Q1/Q3). The interim band is presented as half-years (2H = FY − 1H); the balance sheet shows annual year-ends plus a "Latest" column at 31 March 2026. LTM = twelve months to 31 March 2026 = FY2025 + 1Q2026 − 1Q2025. Continuing-operations basis; no discontinued-operations reclassification. FY2022 is flagged non-comparable: it carried the Meituan in-specie distribution (assets held for distribution 147,965) and RMB116.3bn of net investment gains. Headline non-GAAP = the company's own non-IFRS measures (operating profit, profit attributable, diluted EPS — excluding SBC, net investee gains/losses, intangible amortisation, impairments, SSV & CPP and tax effects) and company-defined Adjusted EBITDA (= operating profit − other gains/losses + D&A + equity-settled SBC), used for leverage/coverage. Segment revenue and segment gross profit are disclosed; segment operating profit / EBITDA are not. Projection base = FY2025; FY2026E–FY2030E columns are model estimates.

5.2Key Assumptions & Methodology↑

5.2.1Financial Model Assumptions↑

Base-case projection drivers, calibrated to sell-side consensus (FY2026E revenue +10.2% ≈ RMB828bn) and reflecting the analyst CP3 sign-off: operating margin held ~1pp below the initial draft, and capital expenditure stepped up to 16% of revenue in FY2026E, easing 1pp/yr to 12% by FY2030E for the AI/data-centre build-out. Circular-free construction: interest = rate × opening debt; cash = cash-flow-statement plug; every non-cash item mirrored in an asset/liability roll so the balance sheet balances by construction. Convention: undeployed FCF accumulates as net cash (no forced redeployment), so the net-cash line builds; the DCF values FCFF and adds only current net cash, avoiding double-count.

Assumption Value Basis Type
Segment revenue growth (VAS / Marketing / FinTech&BS / Others), FY2026E +9.5% / +14% / +9% / +5% history + consensus blend (group +10.2%) sourced
Segment growth taper to FY2030E VAS →+6%, Marketing →+10%, FinTech&BS →+10% maturation of base; AI/cloud reaccel assumed
Group gross margin path 56.8% → 58.2% mix shift to high-margin games/ads/video assumed
Operating margin (per CP3 sign-off) 32.5% → 34.6% (~1pp below initial draft) analyst instruction (higher AI opex in G&A) assumed
Capex % of revenue (per CP3 sign-off) 16% FY2026E, −1pp/yr to 12% FY2030E analyst instruction (heavier AI build-out) assumed
D&A % of revenue 9.0% → 9.8% rises with elevated capex base assumed
Effective tax rate 17.5% FY2025 effective rate 17.1% sourced
Working capital AR/AP/deferred-revenue rolled on FY2025 revenue ratios history (negative WC; deferred-revenue float) assumed
Debt & interest total debt held ~flat RMB388bn; 3.8% on gross debt FY2025 balance-sheet debt note sourced
Capital returns dividends +~13–15%/yr (43→71bn); buybacks ~73bn/yr FY2025 policy (div 37.5bn +30%; buyback 73.3bn) sourced
DCF terminal (proposed, Gate B) WACC 9.0% (Rf 2.0% / ERP 6.5% / β 1.10); Gordon g 3.0%; implied exit 10.0x EV/Adj.EBITDA China 10Y Rf; long-run nominal GDP cap on g assumed

5.2.2Comps / Relative-Valuation Methodology↑

Peer set: core = 9988.HK (Alibaba), 9999.HK (NetEase), 1024.HK (Kuaishou) — the three battlegrounds (diversified platform/cloud, games, short-video ads), all with clean positive-EBITDA data; read-across = 3690.HK (Meituan), PDD, 9888.HK (Baidu). Every multiple is computed from inputs (EV = mkt cap + net debt; EV/EBITDA, NTM P/E, P/FCF, div & FCF yields) — no vendor multiples lifted. Sourcing: Alpha Vantage COMPANY_OVERVIEW was daily-rate-limited on the run date, so peer market data is from Yahoo Finance (yfinance); the subject's LTM Revenue, Adjusted EBITDA, net cash and FCF come from the step-4 model. Currency: comps table in USD with per-field FX because Yahoo reports these China names' price/market-cap/EPS in trading currency (HKD, or USD for PDD) but revenue/EBITDA/net-debt/FCF in reporting currency (RMB/CNY) — trading fields at HKD→USD 0.1275, reporting fields at CNY→USD 0.1472; multiples are FX-invariant. Football field built separately in HKD (subject trading currency) at CNY/HKD 1.155. EBITDA basis: subject Adjusted (model, non-IFRS) vs peers reported (Yahoo) — modestly flatters the subject's relative multiple (Tencent's reported-basis EV/EBITDA is ~9.7x vs the 9.1x Adjusted shown). Yahoo cash figures for these cash-rich names often exclude short-term investments, understating net cash and biasing peer EV/EBITDA high.

5.3DCF Assumptions, WACC Build & Output Detail↑

Dual FCFF + FCFE DCF (deterministic engine). 5-year explicit window FY2026E–FY2030E on the Gate-B-approved model series, RMB mn. WACC build: Ke = Rf 2.0% (China 10-yr govt, RMB basis) + β 1.10 × ERP 6.5% = 9.15%; after-tax Kd = 3.8% × (1−17.5%) = 3.13%; target debt weight 2.5% (token — Tencent is net cash; reproduces the Gate-B WACC) → WACC 9.0%. Beta 1.10 is conservative vs 0.74 observed. Terminal: Gordon, g 3.0% (below China long-run nominal GDP and below terminal-year revenue growth 8.1%). Bridge: EV RMB 4,532bn = PV explicit FCFF 943bn + PV terminal 3,589bn; + net cash 146,860 → equity 4,679bn / 9,218mm diluted shares = RMB 507.6 (HKD 586 at 1.155). Scenarios flex only revenue growth and EBIT margin, calibrated to the model's scenario terminals (bear/base/bull FY30E revenue 1,030,972 / 1,157,696 / 1,267,798; adj-EBITDA 485,588 / 552,220 / 611,078), WACC & g held. Diagnostics: TV 79% of EV; implied exit EV/EBITDA 10.7x reported (10.0x adj) ≈ current trading; FCFF-vs-FCFE reconciliation gap 9.4% (net-cash effect). Excluded from bridge (both conservative): strategic investment portfolio ~RMB 985bn; minority interest ~RMB 89bn → net understatement ~RMB 896bn (~HKD 112/sh). Reverse-DCF: current HKD 421 = RMB ~365/sh, below the entire WACC × g grid; reproduced only at ~11% WACC (~2pp risk premium) or sub-1% terminal growth, or sub-bear operating assumptions.

Component Value
Risk-free rate (Rf, China 10-yr, RMB) 2.0%
Equity risk premium 6.5%
Beta (levered; 0.74 observed) 1.10
Cost of equity (Ke) 9.15%
After-tax cost of debt 3.13%
Target debt weight 2.5%
WACC 9.0%
Terminal method / growth Gordon / 3.0%
Tax rate 17.5%
Bear / Base / Bull revenue CAGR (FY25–30E) 6.5% / 9.0% / 11.0%
Enterprise value RMB 4,532bn
Equity value (FCFF) RMB 4,679bn
Value per share (FCFF) RMB 507.6 / HKD 586
FCFE cross-check per share RMB 459.7 / HKD 531
TV % of EV 79%
Implied exit EV/EBITDA (reported / adj) 10.7x / 10.0x
FCFF vs FCFE reconciliation gap 9.4%

5.4Peer Set Construction↑

Ticker Name Tier Rationale Basis notes
9988.HK Alibaba Group core Closest large-cap China platform — cloud, advertising, fintech; key cloud + commerce-ads + AI comp reported EBITDA (Yahoo); CNY reporting, HKD trading; Yahoo net cash understated
9999.HK NetEase core Cleanest read on the China games segment (#2 domestic); high-margin, cash-generative reported EBITDA (Yahoo); games-only, no ads/fintech/cloud breadth
1024.HK Kuaishou core Direct comp for Video Accounts (short video + closed-loop ads) reported EBITDA (Yahoo); single-platform, smaller scale
3690.HK Meituan read-across Consumer super-app, local-services ads/commerce; engagement-to-monetisation analogue negative TTM EBITDA → EV/EBITDA & P/FCF n/m; thin margins
PDD PDD Holdings read-across High-margin, ads-led e-commerce; growth/margin benchmark reported EBITDA (Yahoo); Temu/international losses & US-listing distort; large net cash
9888.HK Baidu read-across Search advertising + AI cloud; AI-infrastructure read-across reported EBITDA (Yahoo); search-centric, smaller; AI-cloud transition

5.5Data Gaps & Open Items↑

Item Why it matters Where flagged
Segment operating profit/EBITDA & margin (VAS, Marketing, FinTech & BS) Tencent reports profitability at group level only; prevents a segment profit bridge and segment-level valuation Company Overview — Key Reporting Segments
Holding-by-holding investee list and fair values Needed to size the SOTP/stub; only aggregate listed (RMB 547bn)/unlisted (RMB 365bn) disclosed Company Overview — Collateral / Other Risks
Games gross receipts vs recognised revenue by title Deferred-revenue timing drives quarterly games optics (e.g., 1Q26 Spring Festival shift) Company Overview — Key Reporting Segments

Advertiser/customer concentration Revenue durability and bargaining power in Marketing Services Company Overview — Customers
Capex split (AI/GPU vs core infrastructure) Sizes the AI investment cycle and near-term margin drag Company Overview — AI Considerations / Cost Drivers

  • Omdia via Light Reading — China cloud infrastructure share Q3 2025; CIW — Alibaba Cloud IaaS share 2025 (accessed 2026-06-29)

Financials

  • Historical financials transcribed from company filings (FY2023/FY2025 annual reports, 1H2025 interim report, FY2025/1Q2026 results announcements) — no citation per house style. One third-party source for base-case calibration: sell-side consensus and forward estimates from stockanalysis.com (Tencent 0700.HK forecast, retrieved 29 June 2026) — FY2026E revenue ≈ RMB828bn (+10.2%), non-IFRS EPS ≈ 30, "Strong Buy" (45 analysts), average target HKD 708 vs HKD 421 spot.

Valuation

  • Peer market data (price, mkt cap, revenue, EBITDA, net debt, FCF, forward EPS, dividend) — Yahoo Finance via yfinance (accessed 2026-06-30)
  • Subject price, 52-week range & market snapshot — stockanalysis.com (Tencent 0700.HK) (accessed 2026-06-29)
  • FX CNY/HKD 1.155 & per-field USD conversion (CNYUSD 0.1472, HKDUSD 0.1275) — Google Finance / Wise (accessed 2026-06-30)
  • RFR — China 10-yr government bond yield ~2.0% (RMB risk-free used in WACC) (accessed 2026-06-30)

Technical Analysis

  • Price and indicator data from Yahoo Finance via yfinance (symbol 0700.HK; 1-year daily, 1,474 bars). Indicators: EMA(100), Bollinger Bands (20/2σ), Wilder RSI(20), MACD(12/26/9), Volume vs 20-day average. Signal: regime classifier → 4-action tree. Read as of 2026-06-30 (accessed 2026-06-30).

6Disclosures↑ Contents

Analyst certification. The views expressed in this report accurately reflect the analyst's personal views about the subject security and issuer. No part of the analyst's compensation was, is, or will be directly or indirectly related to the specific recommendation or views expressed.

Rating definitions. This report uses a valuation-based entry scale, not a price target:

  • Attractive — intrinsic value is materially above the current price and/or the shares are undervalued on relative multiples and FCF yield versus cost of capital.
  • Fair Value — intrinsic value approximates the current price; risk/reward is balanced.
  • Expensive — intrinsic value is below the current price and/or the shares are richly valued on relative multiples.

Entry timing (accumulate / starter buy / wait / avoid adding) is a separate, technically-driven overlay and does not change the valuation rating. This process sets no price target.

Important disclosures. This is an initiation of coverage. Figures are sourced from company filings and the third-party providers named in the References; market data is as of the dates stated. Financials are in RMB (IFRS); price, market capitalization and per-share valuation context are in HKD, converted at CNY/HKD 1.155. Multiples for the subject use company Adjusted (non-IFRS) EBITDA; peer multiples use reported EBITDA. The intrinsic valuation excludes Tencent's strategic investment portfolio and does not deduct minority interest.

Disclaimer. This report is for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice, an offer, or a solicitation to buy or sell any security. It is not tailored to any recipient's circumstances. Forward-looking estimates are inherently uncertain and actual results may differ materially. The author may have no position in the securities mentioned. Not investment advice.

Sources & notes

Initiating-coverage research compiled from the company's filings, the data room and the analysis sections. Figures are as stated in the underlying sections. Not investment advice.
